headlines.title: CHANGES FOR JUDICIARY ARE ASKED BY BAR
headlines.subtitle: Reforms Are Urged by Texarkana Lawyers-Resolution Is Sent to Colquitt.
headlines.fullText: TEXARKANA, Aug. 20,-Reforms in the Judiciary were urged at a meeting of the Texarkana Bar Association yesterday afternoon. A resolution was adopted to be presented to Governor Colquitt. It said in part: "In view of the fact that a special session of the legislature is to convene at an early date, we respectfully call your attention to the necessity for some legislation that will relieve our courts of civil appeals. It is so well known that this court is years behind with its docket and that it is getting further behind with each term. The law enacted by the last legislature, which was supposed to give that court relief, not only failed of its purpose, but has materially added to the work of that court. Instead of restricting, it has enlarged its appellate jurisdiction. We submit that the delay brought about by this condition has become serious, and in some instances amounts to a denial of justice. This is especially true with poor litigants, who must await the action of the court of last resort before obtaining the relief for which they were compelled to sue, or submit to unfair and often ruinous compromises."

headlines.title: WHEAT ADDED TO WAREHOUSE SPECIAL BILL
headlines.subtitle: Tax of Ten Cents Per Bale Is Provided and Bill Will Come Up Monday.
headlines.fullText: AUSTIN, Aug. 20,-With the finishing touches to the emergency warehouse bill yesterday afternoon, a provision added at the conference will allow the storing of wheat and the issuance of receipts the same as for cotton, it being possible to make these receipts negotiable as deposits at the banks. Another added feature is a tax of 10 cents per bale on all cotton in Texas to provide funds for carrying on the emergency warehouse system. The emergency bill will give cities and counties funds for the erection of warehouses, enabling them to hold local cotton at home and issue receipts. The measure is now ready for introduction at the special session.

headlines.title: CHIEF SAM'S SHIP CLEARS FOR AFRICA
headlines.fullText: GALVESTON, Aug. 20 .- The steamship Liberta of the Akim Trading Company, which is promoting the "back to Africa" movement among Oklahoma and Texas negroes under the direction of Alfred C. Sam, cleared today for numerous ports in Africa, che is expected to sail tomorrow. On board the ship, besides "Chief" Sam, are sixty passengers. That the passenger list is so small is due to a warning issued to Chief Sam a few days ago by the British consul, who informed the leader that those in the party would be considered only as a committee of inspection, with their remaining in Africa to be determined by the authorities there. The Liberia carries a complete cargo of lime, cement, building material and groceries The clearance papers indicate the Liberia will call at various African ports. The Liberia arrived here last March from Portland, Me., and found approximately 600 negroes awaiting a chance to go to Africa. Several hundred of the colonists had been in Galveston for six months when the Liberia arrived. Many of them have returned to their former homes in Oklahoma.

headlines.title: Club Insists on Washing Face of Courthouse Clock
headlines.fullText: That they have the face of the courthouse clock washed and lighted so that it may be discernible after dark is the request made of the county commissioners in a letter received Thursday from the Northeast Side Improvement Club, through its secretary, H. W. Orr. The letter recites that the members of the club take great pride in the courthouse and would like to have its custodians do likewise.

headlines.title: Rattlesnake Egg Hatches Out on Coffee Roaster
headlines.fullText: Special to The Star-Telegram MUSKOGEE, Okla, Aug. 20. Miles Monahan while working on the Midland Valley railroad in Western Arkansas, discovered a nest full of rattlesnake eggs, thirteen in all. He brought them home and placed one of the eggs on the top of a coffee roaster where the sun would strike the eggs. Only one of the eggs hatched but it turned out a snake of the black diamond variety, as lively as any snake hatched in its natural environment. The snake was fourteen inches long when it came out of the shell.

headlines.title: Lurid Language Seems Justified in This Instance
headlines.fullText: When is a man justified in swearing? North Side Park Policeman George Beall debated this question with himself for a while Thursday. Then he walked into the police station and ordered the release of a man whom he had locked up on a charge of swearing. He decided that the man was fully justified in using the cuss words he did. Here are the facts: This man lay down in the shade of a tree in Marine Park Wednesday and went to sleep. While he was quietly snoozing small boys slipped up and took off his shoes and tied the strings to his suspenders and bound his feet securely together with them. The man woke up. Then for the next few minutes the leaves on the trees seemed to bend to the flow of abuse and profanity. The park officer came along and arrested the man. Later he began to reflect, and as a result he walked around to the police station and told Sergeant Howell to release the man, who had been a prisoner four hours.

headlines.title: HOKE SMITH WINS IN GEORGIA PRIMARY
headlines.subtitle: Governor Slaton Has Advantage in Contest to Fill Unexpired Term of Senator Bacon.
headlines.fullText: ATLANTA, Ga., Aug. 20. - Complete returns from all but four Georgia counties early today showed conclusively that Senator Hoke Smith was nominated for the United States senate yesterday in the state-wide Democratic primary over his opponent, former Governor Joseph M. Brown, by a majority of more than 100,000 votes. In the 145 counties heard from, Senator Smith has 125,000 votes and Brown 25,000. Senator Smith will command 345 votes in the state convention and Brown 16. Figures in the race for the nomination for United States senator to fill the unexpired term of the late United States Senator A. O. Bacon gave Governor John M. Slaton the advantage over his opponent, Thomas W. Hardwick. In the race for gubernatorial nomination, Judge N. E. Harris gained rapidly upon Dr. L. G. Hardman and now leads by a vote of 140 to Hardman's 135 and 24 for J. Randolph Anderson.

headlines.title: MONEY IS TO BE FURNISHED TO BUY SEED
headlines.subtitle: Banker Gives Cotton Men Assurance That All They Need Is Ready Market.
headlines.fullText: DALLAS, Aug. 20 .- Whenever a market is found for cotton seed products the banks will advance the money for the purchase of seed and its conversion into finished products, according to Nathan B. Adams, cashier of the American Exchange Bank of Dallas who appeared at a meeting of North Texas cotton seed men yesterday afternoon. The result of the meeting will be a movement among the cotton seed men of North Texas to organize. Ways of handling the cotton seed crop were discussed.

headlines.title: OKLAHOMANS FIGHTING CATTLE SHIPMENT RULE
headlines.subtitle: Order of Animal Industry Bureau Favors Fort Worth Is Claim-Appeal to Congressmen.
headlines.fullText: OKLAHOMA CITY, Aug. 20."-Officials of the Oklahoma National Stock Yards Company and the Oklahoma Livestock Exchange have asked Oklahoma congressmen in Washington to use their influence in having modified an order from the bureau of animal industry concerning the movement of cattle from Mexico. Sometime ago an order was made preventing the movement of Mexican cattle from tick-infested ranges above the quarantine lines, excepting they could be sent as far as Fort Worth, dipped and held sixty days. This order works to the disadvantage of all meat packing centers, except Fort Worth.

headlines.title: Priests Banished From Mexico.
headlines.fullText: NEW ORLEANS, Aug. 20.-Sixteen missionary priests of the Order of Sons of the Sacred Heart, who were banished from Mexico by the constitutionalists, have arrived here en route to San Antonio, where they will remain until assigned to other work. Padre Felix A. Capede has charge of the party.

headlines.title: 70 Varieties of Corn From Same Field Are Shown
headlines.fullText: GREENVILLE, Texas, Aug. 20.-Seventy varieties of corn that had been grown in the same field, cotton from all parts of the world and a variety of other products were shown at the annual fair here, it being Agricultural Day. The exhibit was under the direction of Demonstration Agent J. H. Erickson. Today is Educational and Better Babies Day. The races proved a good feature. In the 2:25 pace, best three in five, Grey Buck won first, D. L. second. In the 2:17 trot, Eunice Strong won first, Loom Up second, Colonel third. Time-2:05 1/2, 2:04 1/2. In the running race, one mile, Sig won first, Rabbit second, Miss Ferguson third.

headlines.title: God With Strong, Declares Huerta, of European War
headlines.fullText: LONDON, Aug. 20.-Victoriano Huerta, former dictator of Mexico, was asked for his opinion of the European war. He called for pen and paper and wrote: "On the present war in Europe I should not speak. All that can be said is what I have already said in the book of a lady, which is as follows: 'God is always with the strongest-that is to say: The strongest is always in the grace of God. It is for this that one is the strongest.'" Then Gen. Huerta closed his lips tightly and made the sign of the cross as if putting the seal on his utterance. He said in English: "Excuse me. Nothing more." Few people at the respectable, unostentatious hotel frequented by middle class provincial merchants where Huerta is staying know that the quiet, sad-looking family man formerly was ruler and master of a great American republic.

headlines.title: INDIANS WILL SUE U. S. FOR $2,000,000
headlines.subtitle: Attorney for Creeks Instructed to Begin Action to Get Balance Due.
headlines.fullText: MUSKOGEE, Okla, Aug. 20 .- R. C. Allen, national attorney for the Creeks, has been notified that congress has authorized him to bring suit in the court of claims against the United States on behalf of the Creek Indians to recover approximately $2,000,000, which is the balance due them under their treaty with the United States for equalization of allotments. The treaty provided that each Indian should be paid enough money to bring his allotment up to $1,040. The Creeks had money enough to equalize up to $800 and not more. The court of claims is to be asked to render judgment against the United States for enough to make up the additional $240 for each of the Indians entitled to equalization money. Allen announced that he would immediately file the suit.

headlines.title: Cowboy Cafeteria Will Be Feature of Reeves Fair
headlines.fullText: PECOS, Texas, Aug. 20.-A cowboy cafeteria will be a feature of the Reeves county fair, the dinner to be served on the ground. The fourth annual fair will open on Sept. 10. A large number of entries have already been assured for the departments. Larger purses are being offered this year than ever before, and the races here will be run in a circuit, continuing them through the fairs at Midland, Big Spring and Abilene. The Pecos Alfalfa Growers' Association will build an alfalfa palace as one of the features.

headlines.title: GATHERING CROP IS PROBLEM AT MERKEL
headlines.subtitle: Country Will Need 1,000 Extra Workers to Keep up With Farmers.
headlines.fullText: MERKEL, Texas, Aug. 20.-The recent general rains which visited the central west so increased the cotton prospects that the farmers in this section are beginning to worry over the problem of gathering the enormous crop that is practically assured. Many farmers say the Merkel country will need 1,000 extra cotton pickers in order to keep up with the picking as fast as it opens.

headlines.title: NEGRO FINED $32.95 FOR CURSING WHITE
headlines.subtitle: Abuse Started When Insurance Collector Went to House on East Tenth.
headlines.fullText: Isaac Scannell, a negro, was tried in police court Thursday morning on a charge of cursing H. C. Campbell, an insurance collector, and fined $32.95. Campbell said he went to a house on East Tenth street Wednesday to collect an account from a negro woman. Scannell said he did not remember cursing Campbell. He admitted that he escaped from the city workhouse a few weeks ago.

headlines.title: ROTAN ORGANIZES TO GATHER COTTON CROP
headlines.subtitle: Biggest Yield in History of Section Calls for Outside Facilities.
headlines.fullText: ROTAN, Texas, Aug. 20.-With the object of getting outside labor to gather the largest cotton crop in the history of this territory, 10,000 to 12,000 bales, and the establishment of a warehouse to store surplus cotton, farmers and business men of this section have organized a Farmers and Business Men's Association. For the first time in many years the farmers are more interested in getting their crops gathered than in the condition of the crop.

headlines.title: 1,500 TEXAS W. O. W. ARE IN COMPETITION
headlines.subtitle: Uniform Companies Engage in Drills at Galveston-Many Are Winners at National Events.
headlines.fullText: GALVESTON, Aug. 20.-The uniform rank companies of the Woodmen of the World opened their competitive drills this morning, in which 1,500 men will participate. Lieutenants Charles A. King and Henry J. Damm of the Nineteenth Infantry, U. S. A., at Fort Crockett, and Col. C. L. Mather of Omaha are serving as judges for today's drills. Several of the companies are winners in national events and the rivalry for that reason is keen. Nine companies are entered in the class A drills, five in the class B drills and seventy-three in individual company drills.

headlines.title: SUGGESTS PARK BE INCLOSED IN LEVEE
headlines.subtitle: Coming Resident of Fort Worth Believes It Would Prevent Portion of Floods in the Flat.
headlines.fullText: J. M. Gilbert of Austin, who says he will become a resident of Fort Worth soon, has written a letter to the city commission suggesting that the levee be rebuilt to inclose Trinity Park. This, he suggests, would not endanger the Holly water plant and would prevent a portion of the floods in the flat due to the gap in the levee opposite the water plant. Gilbert wrote that he had made similar suggestions to the levee board.

headlines.title: SMALL BOATS TO BE ALLOWED UPON LAKE
headlines.subtitle: No Vessels of Any Kind Having Drainage Into Water Will Be Permitted on Reservoir.
headlines.fullText: Small boats will be allowed on Lake Worth after the conduit lines are in operation, according to a rule the city commission will adopt Saturday. While the commission has not noted on the rules to be adopted Saturday will follow the policy given to the Star-Telegram. Boats with drainage into the water will not be permitted on the lake. Smaller craft such as the skiffs and canoes already on the lake will be permitted to use the lake all the time without rental. The big boats and launches will be allowed on the lake until the conduit line is ready for operation and this may be six months or more.

headlines.title: Warehouse Arranged.
headlines.fullText: TEXARKANA, Aug. 20.-To provide a suitable place for storing cotton until it is marketable, the Texarkana Board of Trade has made arrangements with John F. Hunter to build a $10,000 warehouse.

headlines.title: Fire at Belton.
headlines.fullText: BELTON, Texas, Aug. 20, Fire destroyed the Rock Alliance building last night and contents with a loss of $10,000; partly insured. The Woodman hall adjoining was damaged.
